Okay, so I need to figure out whether it's legally valid for a man to leave all his money to his widow's sister, whom he plans to marry next year. Hmm, this seems a bit complicated. Let me break this down step by step.

First, the man is currently married to his wife. The wife has passed away, and now he's thinking about marrying her sister. But he's left all his money to his widow's sister (his future sister-in-law) in his will. The question is whether this is legally valid.

Let me think about the legal concepts involved here. First, there's the issue of testamentary capacity. The man must have mental capacity when making the will for it to be valid. But I don't think that's the main issue here. The main issue seems to be about the relationship of the beneficiary and the legality of the marriage.

So, the man is married to his current wife, and he leaves money to his wife's sister. That's fine if his wife is still alive, but in this case, the wife has passed away. So the man is now a widower, and he's planning to marry his late wife's sister. If he marries her, then his late wife's sister becomes his new wife. But in his will, he left all his money to his late wife's sister. So if he marries her, then the beneficiary becomes his wife. That might complicate things.

Wait, but in some jurisdictions, you can't marry your deceased spouse's sister. Or is that not a legal bar? Also, if someone leaves their money to their future spouse, that might be a problem if the will is executed before the marriage. Because, in most jurisdictions, a will can only be executed for someone who is alive at the time of the testator's death. So if the man marries his late wife's sister, and then the man dies after the marriage, then his wife (his new wife) is the beneficiary. But if he dies before the marriage, then the will would have been executed to someone who was not his spouse at the time of his death, but his future spouse. But in that case, is that allowed?

Also, some jurisdictions have anti-marriage clauses in wills. For example, if you leave money to someone, and then they marry your heir, then the bequest might be void. Or maybe the opposite. But in this case, the man is the testator, and he's marrying his future beneficiary. So if he leaves money to his future wife (his late wife's sister), and then they get married, does that affect the validity of the will?

Wait, but if he marries his late wife's sister, is that even legal? In some places, marrying your deceased spouse's sister is a criminal offense. For example, in some jurisdictions, incest laws prohibit that. So if it's illegal for him to marry his late wife's sister, then the marriage is void or voidable, and maybe that has implications for the will.

So let me summarize the possible issues:

1. Is the marriage between the man and his late wife's sister legally allowed? If not, then the marriage is void, and perhaps the will is invalid if it's contingent on that marriage.

2. If the marriage is legal, then the woman becomes his wife, and the will leaves money to his wife, which is a valid bequest. But if the will was made before the marriage, and he marries her after making the will, then the will bequest to her is valid because she's his wife at the time of his death.

3. If the will was made before the marriage and he dies before the marriage, then the bequest to his future wife (his late wife's sister) would still be valid if she is alive and a legal beneficiary.

4. Also, some jurisdictions may have laws about a will that disinherits a spouse unless stated otherwise. But in this case, the man is leaving everything to his future wife, so if they are married, then she is his spouse and the beneficiary. But if they are not married, then she is his sister-in-law.

But the question is whether the will is valid, not the marriage. The question says "Is this legally valid?" referring to the man leaving all his money to his widow's sister, whom he plans to marry next year.

So regardless of whether the marriage is legal or not, the will itself is a legal document. But if the marriage is illegal, then the will may be invalid if it's contingent on the marriage. But if the will is not contingent on the marriage, then it's valid.

If the marriage is illegal, then the will may be invalid because it's against public policy to have a will that facilitates an illegal marriage.

Or if you leave your estate to someone and then they marry a relative, the bequest may be void. But in this case, the man is the one marrying the beneficiary.

But the key issue is the legality of the marriage. If the marriage is illegal, then even if the will is valid, the marriage may be void, which could have other legal consequences. But the will itself is a separate matter.

In conclusion, the legality of the will depends on several factors:

1. Whether the man had testamentary capacity when making the will.

2. Whether the will was properly executed according to the formalities of wills in the jurisdiction.

3. Whether the marriage to the woman is legal in the jurisdiction. If it's illegal, then the marriage is void, and any will that is contingent on the marriage may be invalid. If the marriage is legal, then the will is valid as it leaves money to a permissible beneficiary.
